title: Event 1 — FCAJ Community Day
content:
Summary Report: FCAJ Community Day Event Information Event Name: FCAJ Community Day
Date & Time: 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM, Saturday, May 23, 2026
Location: Bitexco Financial Tower, Floor 26, 2 Hai Trieu Street, Sai Gon Ward, Ho Chi Minh City
Role: Attendee
Event Schedule Time Session Speaker 8:30 – 9:00 AM Settle into your seat at Floor 26 — 9:00 – 9:30 AM Context Is Everything: Making AI Actually Work for You Tinh Truong 9:30 – 9:45 AM Friendly AI Assistant with Amazon QuickSight Q Anh Pham 9:45 – 10:25 AM From Edge To Origin: CloudFront as Your Foundation Thinh Nguyen 10:25 – 10:55 AM 36 hrs with LotusHacks – Building UTMorpho from Idea to Reality Team VIB 10:55 – 11:00 AM Break — 11:00 – 11:30 AM Non-Determinism of “Deterministic” LLM Settings Duc Dao 11:30 – 12:00 PM Enterprise-Grade Multi-Agent System: The Case of Startup Credit Scoring Vy Lam Session Highlights Context Is Everything: Making AI Actually Work for You — Tinh Truong Why AI fails without context & what “context” really means From prompts to memory: how AI is evolving (Second AI Brain concept) How better context leads to better results (practical mindset & tips) Career insights & how students can start building with AI + Q&A Friendly AI Assistant with Amazon QuickSight Q — Anh Pham Quick Chat Agent: AI assistants for exploring data and analyzing insights Quick Flows: Create intelligent workflows with natural language — no coding required Quick Spaces: Shared collaborative spaces that turn individual insights into team knowledge Quick Sight: Build dashboards and reports from raw data using natural language From Edge To Origin: CloudFront as Your Foundation — Thinh Nguyen Amazon CloudFront for every workload Cost optimization with Amazon CloudFront Security capabilities Enhanced reliability and performance with Amazon CloudFront 36 hrs with LotusHacks – Building UTMorpho from Idea to Reality — Team VIB Why the team joined LotusHacks From Zero to Idea — The Brainstorming Journey Defining the Problem & Shaping UTMorpho Building Under Pressure — 36-Hour Development Sprint Challenges, Failures & Turning Points UTMorpho — Product Overview & Demo Key Learnings & What’s Next Non-Determinism of “Deterministic” LLM Settings — Duc Dao How LLMs choose the next token Assumption: Temperature=0 guarantees determinism Reality: Inference optimizations say otherwise Practical impacts & mitigation strategies Enterprise-Grade Multi-Agent System: The Case of Startup Credit Scoring — Vy Lam The structural mismatch between banking systems and startup data Single Agent: When to and When Not To The Multi-Agent Paradigm Blueprint of a Virtual Credit Committee Guardrails & Compliance Operational ROI & Implementation Roadmap Key Takeaways AI context is everything: The quality of AI output is directly proportional to the quality of the context it receives. Building a structured “Second Brain” approach to prompting is a key skill for working effectively with AI. Amazon QuickSight Q brings natural language to data analytics, enabling non-technical users to generate insights without writing code — a big step toward democratizing data. CloudFront is more than a CDN: It can serve as a security layer, cost optimization tool, and performance foundation for every workload, not just static assets. Hackathon mindset: The UTMorpho team’s 36-hour sprint showed how structured thinking (problem definition → MVP → iteration) under pressure can produce a working product. Failure and pivoting are part of the process. LLMs are not deterministic even at Temperature=0: Hardware parallelism and floating-point non-determinism mean “deterministic” settings are a useful approximation, not a guarantee. Systems depending on exact reproducibility need additional mitigation strategies. Multi-agent systems for compliance: Using a “Virtual Credit Committee” of specialized agents with guardrails shows how AI can be applied responsibly in high-stakes financial domains. Personal Experience Attending the FCAJ Community Day was an inspiring experience. Meeting AWS practitioners, builders, and fellow interns in person — at the iconic Bitexco Financial Tower — made the event feel significant and memorable.
What stood out most was the diversity of the sessions: from foundational AI mindset and cloud infrastructure, to a live hackathon story and cutting-edge multi-agent architecture. Each session gave me a different lens to think about cloud and AI.
The talk on LLM non-determinism was particularly eye-opening for me as someone working with serverless backends — it reminded me that assumptions in software (like “deterministic settings = deterministic output”) often break down at scale or under optimization pressure.
Overall, the event reinforced my motivation to keep building on AWS and to stay connected with the growing FCJ community.
Event Photos

title: Introduction
content:
Source Code: Document-Management-System
What is the DMS Project? The Document Management System (DMS) is the final project of the internship program, applying everything learned about AWS Serverless into a single, cohesive, production-grade system.
The system addresses real-world enterprise pain points:
Secure, centralized document storage with version history Role-based access control for upload, view, and share operations Asynchronous upload pipeline that handles large files without bottlenecks Immutable audit trail for all document operations Automatic malware scanning on every uploaded file Test Accounts & Access Website URL: https://dmt3lkrot0e6x.cloudfront.net/
Role Username Password System Admin admin@gmail.com Admin12345@ Employee fin123@gmail.com Test12345.@A Department Admin depart123@gmail.com Test12345.@A Note: This is an internal website, so the UI will not have a self-registration option. Accounts must be granted by the Admin.
System Architecture Diagram AWS Services Used Service Role in the System Amazon Cognito User pool, JWT auth, 3 user groups (EMPLOYEE / DEPT_ADMIN / SYS_ADMIN) API Gateway REST API, Cognito Authorizer, per-endpoint IAM authorization AWS Lambda 12 serverless handlers, Node.js 22, ARM64, X-Ray tracing Amazon S3 QuarantineBucket (upload staging), DocumentsBucket (final storage) Amazon DynamoDB Single-table design, 4 GSIs, PAY_PER_REQUEST billing, PITR enabled Amazon EventBridge Event bus routing S3 events to processing Lambda functions GuardDuty Malware Protection Plan scanning all quarantine uploads Amazon CloudFront CDN for React SPA frontend served from S3 AWS CDK Full IaC deployment in TypeScript CloudWatch + SNS Log retention, metric alarms, email notifications Key Design Decisions Presigned URL for uploads: Files never pass through API Gateway (avoids 10MB payload limit). The frontend gets a presigned PUT URL and uploads directly to S3 QuarantineBucket. Quarantine-first upload: All files are held in QuarantineBucket until GuardDuty clears them. Only CLEAN-tagged files are moved to the production DocumentsBucket. Single-table DynamoDB: All entities (Document, Version, Share, AuditLog, UploadIntent, UserProfile) share one table with composite keys for cost efficiency. Immutable audit log: AuditLog records are append-only, written with attribute_not_exists conditional PutItem to prevent tampering. Least Privilege IAM: Each Lambda function has its own IAM role with only the permissions it needs — no shared service roles.

title: Blog 1
content:
HOW TO BUILD A COST-EFFECTIVE AWS KMS STRATEGY FOR MULTI-TENANT APPLICATIONS During my studies and internship, I realized that when building SaaS applications serving multiple customers (multi-tenant), managing data encryption is only part of the puzzle. The real challenge arises when balancing strong security, reasonable costs, and easy management as the number of tenants increases. Amazon KMS provides powerful encryption tools, but creating a separate key for each tenant and each service can quickly inflate costs and complicate management.
To solve this problem, AWS proposes a centralized key management approach. This solution builds a central key management layer operating above AWS KMS, combined with IAM roles, aliases, AWS STS, and other services. This architecture reduces costs, simplifies management, ensures isolation between tenants, and maximizes KMS’s encryption capabilities.
HIGHLIGHTS OF THE SOLUTION: Centralized key management in a separate account All customer-managed KMS keys for tenants are located in Account A (Centralized key management service). Customer-facing services in Account B are granted temporary access only when needed. This approach avoids scattered keys and helps control the entire key lifecycle (creation, rotation, deletion) in one place. Using aliases and IAM roles for secure key sharing Each tenant has an alias like alias/customer-<tenant-id>. Service B (usually Lambda) receives a JWT containing the tenant ID, then assumes ServiceBRole → assumes ServiceARole in Account A to access the key via the alias. Thanks to AWS STS, permissions are temporary and strictly controlled. Client-side data encryption and secure storage Sensitive data (passwords, API keys, license info, etc.) is encrypted using Boto3 or the AWS Encryption SDK before being stored in DynamoDB or S3. Because each tenant uses their own key, data is fully isolated even when stored in a shared database. Easy to scale for large systems When the number of tenants grows from dozens to thousands or more, the architecture continues to perform well. You simply create a new key and alias in Account A. No code changes are required in customer-facing services. Costs remain manageable since each key only costs about $1-3/month. Compliance with security and operational best practices The solution uses IAM policies with conditions to limit permissions strictly to the alias/customer- pattern, combined with CloudTrail for auditing. This reduces risk and simplifies compliance with security standards. HOW IT WORKS IN PRACTICE Suppose a customer sends sensitive information (password, API key, license, etc.) via Service B.
Service B receives a JWT containing the tenant ID. Lambda (or another service) validates the JWT. Assume Service B Role → Assume Service A Role in Account A. Use the alias (e.g., alias/customer-<tenant-id>) to access the correct key for that tenant. Encrypt data using Boto3 or the AWS Encryption SDK. Store the encrypted data in DynamoDB. Everything runs smoothly, and data is always protected using each tenant’s dedicated key.
ROLE OF AWS SERVICES The architecture is built on the clear coordination of multiple services:
AWS KMS: Provides customer-managed keys for each tenant. IAM & AWS STS: Manage role assumption permissions between accounts. AWS Lambda: Handles requests, validates JWTs, and performs encryption. Amazon DynamoDB / S3: Where encrypted data is stored. Alias: Keeps the code clean and maintainable without hardcoding key IDs. Each component handles a distinct task. As a result, the system is easy to manage, easy to debug, and reduces dependency on a single service.
BUSINESS VALUE Implementing a centralized KMS strategy brings practical benefits:
Significantly reduces key management costs. Simplifies operations and auditing by centralizing keys. Ensures isolation and high security among tenants. Supports rapid scaling as the business grows. Reduces the burden on DevOps teams, eliminating the fear of “key explosion.” Flexible application across multiple services (DynamoDB, S3, EBS, etc.). This is an ideal solution for SaaS applications, enterprise platforms, systems handling sensitive data, or any multi-tenant project on AWS.
CONCLUSION AWS KMS is a robust encryption service, but when used for multi-tenant applications at scale, a smarter approach is needed to control costs and operations. By building a centralized key management service combined with IAM roles, aliases, and role assumption via JWT, we can effectively solve this problem.
This solution demonstrates a common approach in modern AWS architecture: each service focuses on doing one thing well, then they connect to form a flexible, secure, and cost-effective system.
As an intern, I haven’t worked on a real-world multi-tenant KMS task yet, but after reading the original article and diagram, I find this pattern highly worth learning. It gives me a clearer understanding of how to design cloud systems that balance security, cost, and scalability.
Reference: Simplify multi-tenant encryption with a cost-conscious AWS KMS key strategy

title: Blog 2
content:
Amazon Bedrock Baseline Architecture in an AWS Landing Zone: Deployment Platform for Enterprise Generative AI Building an AI application is not overly difficult. However, when an enterprise has dozens of AI applications running concurrently, the challenge is no longer just connecting to an AI model, but managing the entire AI system securely, stably, and with ease of operation.
Through the Amazon Bedrock Baseline Architecture in an AWS Landing Zone, AWS proposes a reference architecture to help enterprises build a Generative AI platform in a centralized manner, ensuring governance, security, and long-term scalability.
Why is a standard architecture for Generative AI necessary? In the experimental phase, each development team might build their own AI applications and connect directly to Amazon Bedrock. However, as the number of applications grows, enterprises face several challenges:
Difficulty managing access permissions. Difficulty tracking AI usage costs. Difficulty controlling data and traffic. Difficulty applying unified security policies. Difficulty performing auditing and compliance. Proposed Architecture Service Network Account: Manages network connections, controls AI access, manages security policies, orchestrates traffic across accounts, and centralizes all AI traffic at a single point. Generative AI Account: Deploys Amazon Bedrock, Bedrock Knowledge Bases, Bedrock Guardrails, Foundation Models, and related AI services; enables centralized governance, cost tracking, and operational standardization. Workload Accounts: Deploys customer chatbots, internal assistants, document processing, RAG applications, and business applications; accesses AI through a centralized control layer rather than connecting directly to Bedrock. Highlights of the Architecture Separates the AI Platform Account and Workload Account. All AI traffic is centrally controlled via VPC Lattice. Amazon Bedrock is deployed as a shared service for the entire organization. Integrates Bedrock Guardrails to enhance content control and security. Aligns with the multi-account model and AWS Landing Zone. Conclusion Amazon Bedrock Baseline Architecture is not simply a technical architecture but a Generative AI governance model at the enterprise level.
By centralizing AI resource management, controlling traffic, enhancing security, and standardizing operational processes, AWS helps enterprises solve the three biggest challenges when deploying AI:
Security. Governance. Scalability. Recommendations For enterprises testing AI: Start small, build governance early, standardize permissions. For enterprises scaling AI: Separate the AI Account, centrally manage AI resources, track usage costs. For large-scale enterprises: Build a shared AI Platform, deploy a Landing Zone, establish centralized governance, and set up AI Governance and AI Security. Reference: Amazon Bedrock baseline architecture in an AWS Landing Zone

title: Blog 3
content:
Emailing Daily Delta Cost Reports in a Multi-Account Environment Using AWS Lambda and Cost Explorer In an AWS multi-account environment, tracking costs is always a challenging task, especially when finance teams or management do not have direct access to the AWS Billing Console.
While researching cost management and automation on AWS, I read a blog post sharing how to automatically send daily delta cost reports in a multi-account environment. I find this solution highly practical because it increases “cost visibility” while significantly reducing manual operations in cloud cost management.
Common Problems AWS Organizations allows managing and consolidating the billing of multiple accounts within the same organization. However, cost reports are usually only available to those with billing access in the management account. This leads to several issues:
Finance teams or management lack AWS access. Sharing cost reports is often a manual process. Difficulty tracking cost changes across accounts. Lack of “cost visibility” within the organization. Cost management processes become more complex with multiple accounts like Production, Non-Production, or Sandbox. Core Solution The solution uses AWS Lambda to automatically generate and email daily “delta cost” reports. “Delta cost” refers to the cost difference between the current day and the previous day, helping to quickly detect abnormal fluctuations in cloud costs. The system leverages native AWS services such as:
AWS Lambda AWS Cost Explorer API Amazon EventBridge Amazon Simple Email Service (SES) What I find interesting is that the entire architecture is serverless and based on managed services, requiring almost no infrastructure management.
Architecture Overview Workflow:
Amazon EventBridge triggers Lambda on a daily cron schedule. Lambda calls the AWS Cost Explorer API to retrieve cost data from accounts in the organization. Lambda calculates the percentage change in cost (delta) compared to the previous day. The data is formatted into an HTML report. Amazon SES sends the email to the list of recipients. Key Benefits Fully automates the cost reporting process. Makes information easily accessible to leadership and finance teams. Operates effectively in a multi-account environment. Supports increased cost visibility across the organization. Easy to deploy using a CloudFormation template. Can be extended to store report history in S3 for long-term analysis. Additionally, this solution helps technical and finance teams collaborate more effectively in optimizing cloud costs following FinOps principles.
Main Deployment Steps Download the CloudFormation template from the original blog post. Configure account IDs and email recipients. Verify emails in Amazon SES. Deploy the stack. Grant Billing permissions to the IAM Role. Configure an EventBridge rule with a cron expression. Once completed, the system will automatically send daily cost reports via email. Personal Thoughts I find this solution quite practical as it addresses a common problem for many teams using AWS: cloud costs increase, but they are not always tracked in time.
The great thing is that the architecture is compact, primarily using native AWS services like Lambda, EventBridge, and SES, so deployment is not overly complex. To me, this is also a clear example of how AWS uses automation to reduce manual tasks in system operations.
Beyond just sending cost reports, I think this model could be expanded by saving history to S3, creating monitoring dashboards, or incorporating alerts when costs rise abnormally.
Reference: Email delta cost usage report in a multi-account organization using AWS Lambda

title: Event 2 — FCAJ Community Day (June)
content:
Summary Report: FCAJ Community Day — June Edition Event Information Event Name: FCAJ Community Day
Date & Time: 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM, Saturday, June 27, 2026
Location: Bitexco Financial Tower, Amazon Q Conference Room (Floor 26), 2 Hai Trieu Street, Sai Gon Ward, Ho Chi Minh City
Ticket: Amazon Q Conference Room (26F)
Role: Attendee
Event Schedule Time Session Speaker 8:30 – 9:00 AM Settle into your seat — 9:00 – 9:25 AM Deep Response Engine: From Detection to Autonomous Resolution — 9:25 – 9:55 AM Voice Agents: Building Human-Like AI Conversations at Scale — 9:55 – 10:20 AM AWS DevOps Agent: Your Always-Available Operations Teammate — 10:20 – 10:45 AM AI-Powered Productivity: Workforce Planning For Enterprise — 10:45 – 11:30 AM Building Secure Private MCP Connection with Amazon Q — Session Highlights Deep Response Engine: From Detection to Autonomous Resolution The complexity wall in modern cloud operations Shift from alert-driven to action-driven systems Deep Response Engine architecture overview Live demo of autonomous incident response Business impact: cost reduction and zero-downtime operations Voice Agents: Building Human-Like AI Conversations at Scale Evolution from IVR and chatbots to AI voice agents Key challenges: latency, accuracy, and natural interaction Amazon Nova Sonic and speech-to-speech foundation model Architecture: telephony, streaming, Bedrock, MCP tools Enterprise use cases, best practices, and live demo AWS DevOps Agent: Your Always-Available Operations Teammate Overview of AWS DevOps Agent Reducing MTTD and MTTR with AI-driven operations Supporting multi-cloud and hybrid environments Bedrock AgentCore and multi-agent reasoning approach Real-world use cases and ECS demo walkthrough AI-Powered Productivity: Workforce Planning For Enterprise HR transformation challenges in modern enterprises Overview of Amazon Q and its HR capabilities Accelerating HR operations with automation Workforce analytics and data-driven insights Strategic workforce planning for enterprise decision-making Building Secure Private MCP Connection with Amazon Q Introduction to Amazon Q as an AI assistant platform MCP (Model Context Protocol) and its role in extensibility Security challenges in MCP-based integrations Configuring Amazon Q VPC private connectivity Demo and real-world implementation insights Key Takeaways From reactive to autonomous: The Deep Response Engine session challenged my mental model of cloud operations — instead of just detecting and alerting, modern systems are moving toward autonomous remediation. This is directly relevant to the DMS project where proactive monitoring and self-healing would reduce operational toil. Voice AI is maturing fast: Amazon Nova Sonic’s speech-to-speech architecture removes the traditional ASR → NLU → TTS pipeline latency. For enterprise use cases like customer service, this reduces interaction friction significantly. AI-driven DevOps reduces MTTD/MTTR: The AWS DevOps Agent session showed how AI can continuously monitor systems across multi-cloud environments and suggest or even execute fixes — a game changer for on-call operations. Amazon Q for HR: Seeing AI applied to workforce planning (not just software development) was eye-opening. The session demonstrated that the same LLM-powered assistant approach generalizes across business domains. MCP security matters: The private MCP connection session highlighted that as AI assistants gain access to more data and tools via MCP, securing those connections (VPC private connectivity, authentication, scoping) becomes critical — especially in enterprise environments. Personal Experience The June edition of FCAJ Community Day felt even more focused than the May event — all five sessions revolved around agentic AI and AI operations, which gave the day a very coherent theme.
What stood out for me was the progression from individual AI tools to multi-agent systems: the Deep Response Engine, AWS DevOps Agent, and MCP sessions all pointed in the same direction — AI is moving from assistive to autonomous, and the infrastructure to support that (Bedrock AgentCore, MCP, VPC connectivity) is maturing rapidly.
Being in the Amazon Q Conference Room named after the product itself added an interesting touch to the experience. The sessions were technical and demo-heavy, which I appreciated as someone actively building with AWS.
Overall, this event reinforced that the skills I am developing during this internship — serverless architecture, IAM, Lambda, DynamoDB — are the exact building blocks that agentic AI systems are built on top of.
Event Photos

title: Prerequisites
content:
Before deploying the DMS project, make sure the following tools and accounts are ready.
1. Required Accounts & Access AWS Account with sufficient permissions to create IAM roles, Lambda, S3, DynamoDB, Cognito, CloudFront, EventBridge, GuardDuty, SNS, CloudWatch, and CDK Bootstrap resources. AWS CLI configured with a profile that has the required permissions. 2. Required Tools Tool Version Purpose Node.js >= 20.x Run CDK and Lambda build npm >= 10.x Package management AWS CDK CLI >= 2.x Deploy infrastructure Git Any Clone the repository Install CDK globally if not already installed:
npm install -g aws-cdk Verify versions:
node --version npm --version cdk --version aws --version 3. Bootstrap CDK (First-time only) CDK Bootstrap creates the required S3 bucket and IAM roles in your AWS account for CDK to store deployment artifacts.
cdk bootstrap aws://<ACCOUNT_ID>/<REGION> Example:
cdk bootstrap aws://123456789012/ap-southeast-1 4. Clone the Repository git clone <your-repository-url> cd DMS npm install 5. Configure Environment Variables Copy the example environment file and fill in your values:
cp .env.example .env The .env.example file contains the required variables:
# Alert email for CloudWatch cost/error notifications DMS_ALERT_EMAIL=your-email@example.com 6. Build Lambda Functions Before deploying, compile the TypeScript Lambda code:
cd aws/functions npm install npm run build cd ../.. 7. Verify Project Structure Make sure the project structure looks like this before deploying:
DMS/ ├── aws/ │ ├── functions/ # Lambda handlers (TypeScript) │ │ └── dist/ # ← Must exist after npm run build │ └── infrastructure/ # AWS CDK stack (TypeScript) ├── frontend/ # React + Vite frontend ├── contracts/ # OpenAPI schemas └── docs/ # Data model & decisions

title: Proposal
content:
Internal Document Management System (DMS) using AWS Serverless 1. Executive Summary The Document Management System (DMS) project is designed to build a secure and automated system for storing, authorizing, and managing the lifecycle of internal documents. By fully leveraging the AWS Serverless ecosystem (Cognito, API Gateway, Lambda, S3, DynamoDB, SQS), the system optimizes operational costs (pay-as-you-go) while ensuring enterprise-level scalability and security.
2. Problem Statement Current Issues:
Storing and sharing internal documents via traditional platforms presents multiple challenges: difficulty in tracing version history, lack of granular access control mechanisms, high risk of sensitive data leakage, and the significant cost of maintaining physical servers 24/7 despite irregular usage patterns.
Proposed Solution:
Build a 100% Serverless architecture. Specifically:
Use Amazon S3 as the document storage space with versioning and encryption enabled. Use DynamoDB to store metadata (creator, timestamp, edit history) with millisecond latency. Handle file upload processes asynchronously using Amazon SQS to avoid bottlenecks during high concurrent user interactions. Implement identity and access management via Amazon Cognito. Return on Investment (ROI):
The system requires zero fixed costs for server management and automatically scales with traffic. The estimated maintenance cost during the development phase is less than $2/month thanks to the AWS Free Tier.
3. Solution Architecture The system utilizes the following core services to build a highly secure and performant data flow:
AWS Services Used:
Amazon Cognito: User authentication and JWT Token issuance. Amazon API Gateway: The single entry point (REST API) for the frontend, directly integrated with Cognito Authorizer. AWS Lambda: Business logic processing (Document CRUD, access authorization). Amazon S3: Secure storage for physical document files (Private S3 Bucket). Amazon DynamoDB: NoSQL database for storing document metadata. Amazon SQS: Asynchronous queue processing for large file uploads or malware scanning tasks. Amazon CloudWatch & GuardDuty: System log monitoring and continuous threat detection. 4. Technical Implementation The project is implemented with the following technical requirements:
Frontend: A simple Web Application (SPA) using React/Next.js communicating with AWS via REST APIs. Backend (Serverless): Lambda functions written in Python/Node.js. Utilizing the boto3 SDK to interact with S3 and DynamoDB. Security: Applying IAM Roles with the Principle of Least Privilege for each individual Lambda function. 5. Roadmap & Milestones The project spans across the last 4 weeks of the internship:
Week 9: Business requirement analysis, system architecture design, and data flow modeling. Week 10: Network setup, IAM authorization, and Amazon Cognito configuration. Developing the login/registration flow. Week 11: Deploying the Serverless Backend (Lambda, API Gateway, DynamoDB, S3) and integrating SQS for asynchronous upload processing. Week 12: Comprehensive testing (Function Test, Load Test, Security Test with GuardDuty), architecture optimization, and handover documentation. 6. Budget Estimation Expected deployment costs are extremely low due to the Pay-as-you-go model.
AWS Lambda: ~$0.00 (within the free tier of 1 million requests/month). Amazon S3: ~$0.15 for storage and outbound data transfer. DynamoDB: ~$0.00 (within the 25GB free tier). API Gateway & Cognito: Free for low internal user volume. Estimated Total Budget: Under $1/month for the testing environment and roughly under $10/month for a medium-scale production environment. 7. Risk Assessment & Contingency Data Leakage Risk: High.
Mitigation: Server-side encryption (S3 SSE) and blocking all Public access to S3. Granting only temporary presigned-URLs to the client. Large File Upload Bottleneck Risk: Medium.
Mitigation: Handling uploads directly via S3 Presigned URLs and using SQS for Lambda to process metadata in the background instead of passing massive payloads through API Gateway. 8. Expected Outcomes Deliver a digitized, secure, and easily traceable document management system for the company. Eliminate document fragmentation across disjointed platforms. Master the design and implementation of Enterprise applications using AWS Serverless Architecture.

title: Week 2 Worklog
content: Week 2 Objectives: Master AWS networking concepts (VPC, Subnet, Route Table). Practice configuring VPCs, Hybrid DNS, VPC Peering, and Transit Gateway. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 2 - Overview of VPC - CIDR, Subnet, Route Table, Public and Private Subnets 27/04/2026 27/04/2026 000003.awsstudygroup.com 3 - Create a new VPC - Configure Public/Private Subnets, Internet Gateway, and Route Table 28/04/2026 28/04/2026 000003.awsstudygroup.com 4 - Learn Hybrid DNS - Familiarize with Amazon Route 53 Resolver, Inbound and Outbound Endpoints 29/04/2026 29/04/2026 000010.awsstudygroup.com 5 - Practice VPC Peering - Connect two VPCs, update Route Tables, and test connectivity 30/04/2026 30/04/2026 000019.awsstudygroup.com 6 - Learn AWS Transit Gateway - Compare Transit Gateway with VPC Peering in multi-VPC architectures 01/05/2026 01/05/2026 000020.awsstudygroup.com Week 2 Achievements: Grasped fundamental knowledge of Virtual Private Cloud (VPC), distinguishing the roles of Public and Private Subnets in secure infrastructure design. Hands-on successfully built a complete VPC, configuring routing via Route Tables and Internet Gateways for outbound internet access. Deepened understanding of Hybrid DNS resolution using Amazon Route 53 Resolver, configuring Inbound and Outbound Endpoints. Successfully deployed network connectivity between VPCs via VPC Peering and understood how AWS Transit Gateway optimizes large-scale network architectures.

title: Deploy Infrastructure with AWS CDK
content:
This step deploys the entire DMS infrastructure to AWS using a single CDK command. The CDK stack provisions all services automatically.
What Gets Deployed The DmsStack creates the following resources in order:
1. Amazon Cognito — User Pool
Pool name: dms-dev Sign-in by email only, self-registration disabled 3 user groups: EMPLOYEE (precedence 30), DEPARTMENT_ADMIN (precedence 20), SYSTEM_ADMIN (precedence 10) Password policy: minimum 12 characters, requires digits, uppercase, lowercase, and symbols Token validity: Access/ID token = 60 minutes, Refresh token = 7 days 2. Amazon S3 — Three Private Buckets FrontendBucket — hosts the compiled React SPA QuarantineBucket — receives all file uploads first; GuardDuty scans here DocumentsBucket — stores only clean, approved documents All buckets block public access; CORS configured on QuarantineBucket for PUT operations 3. Amazon DynamoDB — Single Table
Table name: dms-dev Billing: PAY_PER_REQUEST (no capacity planning needed) Encryption: AWS_MANAGED Point-In-Time Recovery (PITR): enabled TTL attribute: expiresAtEpoch (for auto-expiring UploadIntent records) 4 Global Secondary Indexes (GSI1–GSI4) for various query patterns 4. AWS Lambda → 12 functions visible (Node.js 22, ARM64) Handler Purpose me Get current user profile documents List documents for a user/department document-detail Get a single document with versions document-audit-events Get audit log for a document upload-intents Create presigned PUT URL to QuarantineBucket download-intents Create presigned GET URL from DocumentsBucket document-sharing Share/revoke document access admin-users Create/manage users in Cognito analytics Aggregate storage and activity statistics process-upload EventBridge target: move clean files, write DynamoDB process-upload-dlq Handle failed event deliveries from Dead Letter Queue 5. Amazon EventBridge — Event Routing
Event bus routing S3 ObjectCreated events directly to the processing Lambda Dead Letter Queue: handles failed event deliveries if Lambda processing fails S3 QuarantineBucket sends events to EventBridge on every s3:ObjectCreated:Put under the quarantine/ prefix 6. GuardDuty Malware Protection Plan
Dedicated IAM role grants GuardDuty access to scan QuarantineBucket Files are tagged CLEAN or THREAT_FOUND after scanning 7. Amazon API Gateway — REST API
Cognito Authorizer validates JWT on all protected routes Routes map to Lambda functions by HTTP method and path 8. Amazon CloudFront — CDN for Frontend
Serves the React SPA from FrontendBucket via CloudFront OAC 9. CloudWatch + SNS Alerts
Log groups with 2-week retention (dev) or 3-month retention (production) SNS topic sends email alerts for cost anomalies or Lambda errors Deploy Command Navigate to the infrastructure directory and run:
cd aws/infrastructure npm install npx cdk deploy -c environment=dev -c alertEmail=your-email@example.com Or use the environment variable:
DMS_ALERT_EMAIL=your-email@example.com npx cdk deploy -c environment=dev Verify Deployment After cdk deploy completes, check the Outputs section in the terminal. You should see:
UserPoolId — the Cognito User Pool ID UserPoolClientId — the Cognito App Client ID ApiUrl — the API Gateway endpoint URL CloudFrontUrl — the frontend URL You can also verify in the AWS Console:
CloudFormation → Stack DmsStack-dev → Status: CREATE_COMPLETE DynamoDB → Table dms-dev exists with 4 GSIs S3 → Three buckets created Lambda → 11 functions visible Cognito → User pool dms-dev with 3 groups

title: Event 3 — FCAJ Community Day (May 9)
content:
Summary Report: FCAJ Community Day Event Information Event Name: FCAJ Community Day
Date & Time: 9:00 AM – 12:00 PM, Saturday, May 9, 2026
Location: Bitexco Financial Tower, Floor 26, 2 Hai Trieu Street, Sai Gon Ward, Ho Chi Minh City
Role: Attendee
Event Schedule Time Session Speaker 9:00 – 9:45 AM Hack Your Brain: How to Addict Learning Like Gaming Mr. Huynh Hoang Long (AWS Intern) 9:45 – 10:25 AM Ultimate Prompt Engineering: Enhancing LLM Output Quality Mr. Nguyen Tuan Thinh (AWS Intern) 10:25 – 11:30 AM Career Mindset: Why Aren’t You Working Yet? Mr. Khang Nguyen (Solutions Architect, Cloud Kinetics) 11:30 – 12:00 PM Building Software with AI Agent Frameworks: The BMT Method Ms. Thao (Software Developer, VIB) Session Highlights Hack Your Brain: How to Addict Learning Like Gaming — Mr. Huynh Hoang Long The Dopamine Loop: Dopamine isn’t released when getting a reward, but during the anticipation of it. Social media and games exploit this with instant feedback and unpredictable rewards. The Streak Effect: People are motivated by the fear of losing progress. Keeping a daily tracking calendar (like a Duolingo streak) transforms the fear of losing a consecutive streak into studying momentum. Deceiving the Amygdala: Breaking overwhelming massive tasks into micro-steps (e.g., just creating an AWS account or opening a single file) prevents the brain from freezing up and avoiding hard subjects. Ultimate Prompt Engineering: Enhancing LLM Output Quality — Mr. Nguyen Tuan Thinh Architecture & Project Showcase: The speaker builds a serverless prompt optimization application utilizing Amazon CloudFront and S3 for content delivery, Amazon Cognito for serverless authentication management, API Gateway, AWS Lambda for computing, Amazon Bedrock for accessing foundation models, and DynamoDB for low-latency storage. Structured Prompting: Explains the 7 core components of a production prompt (Role, Instruction, Context, Input, Output Format, Examples, Constraints). Emphasizes using explicit directive language over negative rules. Advanced AI Reasoning: Touches upon how techniques like Chain of Thought (thinking step-by-step), Self-Consistency, and Tree of Thought (exploring thought pathways via BFS/DFS) significantly enhance LLM response accuracy. Career Mindset: Why Aren’t You Working Yet? — Mr. Khang Nguyen AI Amplification Effect: AI doesn’t replace thinking; it amplifies it. If you have poor foundational skills, AI makes you worse by hiding your lack of understanding under beautiful superficial outputs. If you are good, it multiplies your productivity. The Core Foundation & Thought Process: Tech frameworks change daily, but foundational infrastructure and core logic do not. Employers filter for the thought process and the reasoning behind choices rather than just an answer that works. Professional Integrity (Liêm chính): True growth comes from diving deep into edge cases and testing hidden failures, even when managers aren’t looking. Long-term reputation and network investment far outweigh short-term monetary gains. Building Software with AI Agent Frameworks: The BMT Method — Ms. Thao The “Overcoding” Trap: Standard casual prompting for long dev sessions causes LLMs to exceed their context windows, leading to hallucinations and producing broken, unmanageable spaghetti code. The BMT Method: Introduces the open-source BMT Method (BMTM) framework, shifting the focus from writing manual code to writing precise markdown documentation. Multi-Agent Collaborative Ecosystem: The framework splits development stages across dedicated virtual personas (Product Manager Agent, System Architect Agent, Scrum Master Agent, Developer Agent, and Reviewer Agent) that pass highly specific context packets down the line to isolate errors and control code generation states. Key Takeaways Gamifying discipline: To build consistency in deep engineering, you need to create a visual feedback loop of micro-rewards and continuous tracking streaks to counter the long, delayed gratification loop inherent in technical studies. Context isolates hallucination: When building software applications powered by AI, isolating logic boundaries through rigid structures (whether it’s well-designed prompts or breaking systems into isolated agent nodes) reduces the prompt context footprint and avoids degradation. Focus on the “Why”: Senior technical experts and tech companies value the underlying engineering intent, the architectural compromises made, and your grasp of structural basics over code copy-pasted directly out of an LLM canvas. Long-term technical compounding: Success in software and cloud engineering belongs to those looking ahead on a multi-year timeline, choosing teams over isolated solo building, and taking on challenging real-world project trials where failure is guaranteed but deeply educational. Personal Experience Attending the FCAJ Community Day provided an amazing, highly practical look into modern cloud development, prompt workflows, and AI engineering practices. Getting to connect directly with working practitioners like Solutions Architects from Cloud Kinetics and Software Developers from VIB inside Bitexco Financial Tower made the learning experience feel highly grounded in industry reality.
The event structure perfectly spanned both mindset engineering and tactical architectural designs—moving from psychological bio-hacks for better studying, to serverless full-stack deployments on AWS, down to long-term career principles regarding professional integrity and long-horizon compounding.
The deep dive into multi-agent framework architectures was especially eye-opening. It clearly demonstrated that as AI tools take over trivial code-generation tasks, the true value of an engineer shifts dramatically toward system design, precise technical writing, and robust logical decomposition. It serves as an active reminder to keep exploring advanced cloud patterns, building end-to-end multi-tier applications, and engaging deeply with the tech community.
Event Photos

title: Translated Blogs
content:
This section will list and introduce the blogs you have translated. For example:
Blog 1 - How to Build a Cost-Effective AWS KMS Strategy for Multi-tenant Applications This blog introduces a centralized key management approach to build a cost-effective AWS KMS strategy for multi-tenant applications. You will learn how to use a centralized key management layer along with IAM roles, aliases, and AWS STS to ensure strong security, reduce costs, and simplify management for SaaS applications at scale.
Blog 2 - Amazon Bedrock Baseline Architecture in an AWS Landing Zone This blog explores how to build a robust Generative AI deployment platform for enterprises using the Amazon Bedrock Baseline Architecture. You will understand why a standard architecture is necessary for AI governance, security, and scalability, and how separating service networks, AI platforms, and workloads can solve major enterprise challenges.
Blog 3 - Emailing Daily Delta Cost Reports in a Multi-Account Environment Using AWS Lambda and Cost Explorer This blog discusses an automated, serverless solution to generate and email daily delta cost reports across an AWS Organization. By leveraging AWS Lambda, Cost Explorer API, EventBridge, and SES, it enhances cost visibility for finance and management teams while reducing manual reporting efforts, serving as a practical approach to cloud financial management (FinOps).

title: Configure Backend & Test APIs
content:
After the infrastructure is deployed, this step walks through creating the first user, uploading a document, and verifying the full end-to-end flow.
1. Create the First Admin User Since selfSignUpEnabled: false, all users must be created by an administrator.
Go to AWS Console → Cognito → User Pools → dms-dev → Users → Create user:
Email: admin@yourcompany.com Temporary password: Set a strong one (will be changed on first login) After creation, go to Groups tab and add the user to SYSTEM_ADMIN Or use the AWS CLI:
aws cognito-idp admin-create-user \ --user-pool-id <UserPoolId> \ --username admin@yourcompany.com \ --temporary-password "Temp@12345!" \ --user-attributes Name=email,Value=admin@yourcompany.com Name=email_verified,Value=true Name=name,Value="Admin User" aws cognito-idp admin-add-user-to-group \ --user-pool-id <UserPoolId> \ --username admin@yourcompany.com \ --group-name SYSTEM_ADMIN 2. Authenticate and Get JWT Token Use the Cognito USER_PASSWORD_AUTH flow to get an access token:
aws cognito-idp initiate-auth \ --auth-flow USER_PASSWORD_AUTH \ --client-id <UserPoolClientId> \ --auth-parameters USERNAME=admin@yourcompany.com,PASSWORD="YourNewPassword" Copy the AccessToken from the response. You will use this as a Bearer token in all API calls.
3. Test the /me Endpoint curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <AccessToken>" \ https://<ApiUrl>/me Expected response:
{ "userId": "...", "email": "admin@yourcompany.com", "name": "Admin User", "groups": ["SYSTEM_ADMIN"] } 4. Upload a Document (Full Flow) Step 4a — Request a Presigned Upload URL:
curl -X POST \ -H "Authorization: Bearer <AccessToken>" \ -H "Content-Type: application/json" \ -d '{"filename": "report.pdf", "contentType": "application/pdf", "size": 102400}' \ https://<ApiUrl>/upload-intents Response includes:
{ "uploadIntentId": "ui-xxxx", "presignedUrl": "https://s3.amazonaws.com/quarantine-bucket/quarantine/...", "expiresAt": "2026-07-03T14:00:00Z" } Step 4b — Upload directly to S3 using the Presigned URL:
curl -X PUT \ -H "Content-Type: application/pdf" \ --data-binary @report.pdf \ "<presignedUrl>" Step 4c — Verify the pipeline ran automatically:
Go to S3 → QuarantineBucket → verify the file appeared Go to GuardDuty → Malware Protection → verify the scan ran and tagged the file Go to S3 → DocumentsBucket → the clean file should appear here after ~30 seconds Go to DynamoDB → dms-dev → check for a new DOC#... record 5. List Documents curl -H "Authorization: Bearer <AccessToken>" \ https://<ApiUrl>/documents You should see the uploaded document in the response with its documentId, filename, versionNumber, and timestamps.
6. Run the Frontend Locally cd frontend npm install npm run dev Open http://localhost:5173 in your browser. Enter the UserPoolId, UserPoolClientId, and ApiUrl from the CDK outputs to configure the app, then log in with your admin credentials.
7. Explore Frontend Features Once logged in, you can test various features provided by the React SPA:
Download Document: Share Document: Manage Users (System Admin only): Department Management (Department Admin only): Audit Logs & Admin History: Analytics Dashboard:

Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 2 - Review AWS Backup - Standardize the data backup and recovery process on AWS 11/05/2026 11/05/2026 000013.awsstudygroup.com 3 - Configure Backup Vault, Backup Rules, and Resource Assignment according to lab requirements 12/05/2026 12/05/2026 000013.awsstudygroup.com 4 - Use AWS VM Import/Export to migrate Virtual Machines between On-premises and Amazon EC2 13/05/2026 13/05/2026 000014.awsstudygroup.com 5 - Learn Hybrid Storage - Configure File Gateway and create File Shares 14/05/2026 14/05/2026 000024.awsstudygroup.com 6 - Mount File Share on local machine - Learn Amazon FSx for Windows File Server and test data access 15/05/2026 15/05/2026 000025.awsstudygroup.com Week 4 Achievements: Solidified and standardized automated backup and recovery processes for various services on the AWS platform. title: Security & Monitoring
content:
The DMS system applies multiple layers of security and monitoring to protect data and detect anomalies in real-time.
1. IAM — Principle of Least Privilege Each Lambda function has its own dedicated IAM role with only the permissions it needs:
Lambda Permissions Granted documents dynamodb:Query on Documents table (read-only) upload-intents dynamodb:PutItem + s3:PutObject on QuarantineBucket only download-intents dynamodb:GetItem + s3:GetObject on DocumentsBucket only admin-users cognito-idp:AdminCreateUser, AdminAddUserToGroup, etc. process-upload s3:GetObject on Quarantine + s3:PutObject on Documents + dynamodb:PutItem No Lambda function has * permissions or cross-service overprivileged access.
2. S3 — Private Buckets & Presigned URLs All three S3 buckets have Block All Public Access enabled. Documents are never directly accessible via public URLs. Access is granted only through time-limited Presigned URLs generated by Lambda: Upload: Presigned PUT URL (expires in ~15 minutes) Download: Presigned GET URL (expires in ~5 minutes) S3 server-side encryption (SSE-S3) is applied to all stored objects. 3. GuardDuty — Malware Protection The MalwareProtectionPlan resource scans every file uploaded to QuarantineBucket:
File arrives in quarantine/ prefix via Presigned URL. GuardDuty automatically scans the file. GuardDuty tags the S3 object: GuardDutyMalwareScanStatus: CLEAN → safe to process GuardDutyMalwareScanStatus: THREAT_FOUND → discard, log to audit Lambda process-upload reads the tag before moving the file. To view scan results:
Go to GuardDuty → Malware Protection → Protected buckets Or check the S3 object tags directly in the Console 4. Cognito — Role-Based Access Control Three user groups enforce different permission levels in the backend:
Group Access Level EMPLOYEE (precedence 30) Can upload, view, and download their own documents DEPARTMENT_ADMIN (precedence 20) Can manage documents for their department, share with users SYSTEM_ADMIN (precedence 10) Full access — manage users, view analytics, access all documents The group claim is embedded in the JWT token and validated by Lambda at runtime.
5. CloudWatch — Log Monitoring All 11 Lambda functions write structured logs to CloudWatch Log Groups:
Log retention: 2 weeks for dev, 3 months for production X-Ray tracing is enabled on all Lambda functions for end-to-end request tracing To view logs:
Go to CloudWatch → Log Groups Find /aws/lambda/DmsStack-dev-<FunctionName> Use Insights to query: fields @timestamp, @message | sort @timestamp desc | limit 50 6. SNS — Cost & Error Alerts A CloudWatch Alarm is connected to an SNS topic that emails your alertEmail when:
Lambda error rate exceeds a threshold Monthly estimated cost goes above budget To verify alerts are working:
Check SNS → Topics → DmsAlertTopic Confirm your email subscription is Confirmed Check CloudWatch → Alarms to see active alarm definitions

title: Workshop
content:
Document Management System (DMS) on AWS Serverless Overview The Document Management System (DMS) is a cloud-native application built entirely on the AWS Serverless ecosystem. It allows internal users to securely upload, version, share, and audit company documents — without managing any servers.
The system is deployed via AWS CDK (Infrastructure as Code) and integrates the following core AWS services:
Amazon Cognito — User authentication and role-based access control (EMPLOYEE / DEPARTMENT_ADMIN / SYSTEM_ADMIN) Amazon API Gateway — Single REST API entry point with Cognito Authorizer AWS Lambda — Serverless business logic handlers (Node.js 22, ARM64) Amazon S3 — Private document storage (DocumentsBucket) and upload quarantine (QuarantineBucket) Amazon DynamoDB — Single-table design with GSI indexes for metadata, versioning, sharing, and audit logs Amazon EventBridge — Serverless event bus routing S3 events to Lambda Amazon GuardDuty — Malware Protection scanning for uploaded files Amazon CloudFront — CDN delivery for the React frontend Amazon CloudWatch — Centralized log monitoring and cost alerting Amazon SNS — Email alerts for anomalous cost or system errors Architecture The data flow follows this pattern:
The user logs in via the React frontend using Cognito and receives a JWT token. All API calls go through API Gateway, which validates the JWT via Cognito Authorizer. File uploads use Presigned URLs — the frontend uploads directly to QuarantineBucket (S3), bypassing API Gateway. Amazon EventBridge routes an Object Created event to Lambda when a new file lands in the quarantine bucket. GuardDuty Malware Protection scans the file and tags it with CLEAN or THREAT_FOUND. A Lambda worker triggered by EventBridge reads the GuardDuty scan tag, moves clean files to DocumentsBucket, and writes metadata + version records to DynamoDB. Document sharing, audit logs, analytics, and user management are all handled by dedicated Lambda functions. Workshop Content Workshop Overview Prerequisites Deploy Infrastructure with AWS CDK Configure Backend & Test APIs Security & Monitoring with GuardDuty and CloudWatch Clean Up Resources

title: Week 5 Worklog
content: Week 5 Objectives: Learn AWS security standards and resource management via Security Hub, Tagging, Resource Groups, and IAM. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 2 - Learn AWS Security Standards and Security Hub - Enable Security Hub and review Security Score 18/05/2026 18/05/2026 000018.awsstudygroup.com 3 - Learn AWS Tagging - Tag EC2 instances and filter resources by tags on the AWS Console 19/05/2026 19/05/2026 000022.awsstudygroup.com 4 - Learn Resource Groups - Create Resource Groups based on tags and manage resources as groups 20/05/2026 20/05/2026 000027.awsstudygroup.com 5 - Create IAM Users and Policies - Verify authorization mechanisms and the least privilege principle 21/05/2026 21/05/2026 000028.awsstudygroup.com 6 - Create IAM Role for Lambda - Create a Lambda function for automated resource tagging and test results 22/05/2026 22/05/2026 000030.awsstudygroup.com Week 5 Achievements: Enhanced cloud security awareness and applied industry-standard security practices by enabling AWS Security Hub. Learned how to categorize and manage bulk resources scientifically through AWS Tagging strategies and Resource Groups. Reinforced IAM knowledge by practically applying the least privilege principle in real-world permission assignment. Integrated automation into resource management workflows by writing a Lambda Function to automate tagging for EC2 instances.

title: Clean Up Resources
content:
When you are done with the workshop, follow these steps to delete all AWS resources and avoid ongoing charges.
1. Destroy the CDK Stack Run the following command from the aws/infrastructure directory:
cd aws/infrastructure npx cdk destroy -c environment=dev CDK will ask for confirmation:
Are you sure you want to delete: DmsStack-dev (y/n)? y This command will automatically delete:
All 3 S3 buckets (FrontendBucket, QuarantineBucket, DocumentsBucket) and their contents DynamoDB table dms-dev and all data All 12 Lambda functions and their CloudWatch Log Groups API Gateway REST API Cognito User Pool and all users EventBridge Rules and targets CloudFront distribution SNS topic and email subscriptions GuardDuty Malware Protection Plan and IAM role 2. Verify Deletion in AWS Console After the destroy command completes, verify that resources have been removed:
CloudFormation → confirm DmsStack-dev stack no longer exists S3 → confirm the 3 DMS buckets are gone DynamoDB → confirm table dms-dev is deleted Lambda → confirm DMS functions are removed Cognito → confirm user pool dms-dev is deleted 3. Remove CDK Bootstrap (Optional) If you want to fully clean up and remove the CDK bootstrap stack as well:
aws cloudformation delete-stack --stack-name CDKToolkit 4. Check for Remaining Charges After cleanup, verify in AWS Billing → Cost Explorer that no DMS-related charges appear in the next billing cycle. Common items to check:
S3 storage charges DynamoDB read/write units Lambda invocations CloudWatch log storage GuardDuty scan charges Summary Congratulations on completing the DMS Workshop! You have successfully:
Deployed a fully serverless Document Management System on AWS using CDK Configured role-based access control with Amazon Cognito Implemented a secure upload pipeline with S3 Presigned URLs and GuardDuty malware scanning Used a single-table DynamoDB design with 4 GSIs for efficient querying Set up CloudWatch logging, X-Ray tracing, and SNS cost alerts Verified the full end-to-end document upload, scan, and retrieval flow

title: Self-Assessment
content:
During my internship at Amazon Web Services Vietnam Co., Ltd. from April 17, 2026 to July 12, 2026, as a student from Ho Chi Minh City University of Technology (HUTECH), I had the precious opportunity to learn, practice, and apply my academic knowledge into a highly professional real-world working environment.
In this internship, I participated in exploring the AWS ecosystem, writing technical reports, and deploying a Document Management System project using AWS Serverless architecture. Through this process, I significantly improved my technical skills such as cloud architecture design, backend programming, and cloud cost management, as well as soft skills like writing technical documentation, self-learning, and time management.
Regarding my work attitude, I always strived to complete my assigned tasks well, proactively sought out new knowledge, and strictly complied with the company’s regulations.
To objectively reflect on my internship period, I would like to evaluate myself based on the following criteria:
No. Criteria Description Good Fair Average 1 Professional knowledge & skills Understanding of the field, applying knowledge in practice, proficiency with tools, work quality ✅ ☐ ☐ 2 Ability to learn Ability to absorb new knowledge and learn quickly ✅ ☐ ☐ 3 Proactiveness Taking initiative, seeking out tasks without waiting for instructions ☐ ✅ ☐ 4 Sense of responsibility Completing tasks on time and ensuring quality ✅ ☐ ☐ 5 Discipline Adhering to schedules, rules, and work processes ☐ ✅ ☐ 6 Progressive mindset Willingness to receive feedback and improve oneself ✅ ☐ ☐ 7 Communication Presenting ideas and reporting work clearly ☐ ✅ ☐ 8 Teamwork Working effectively with colleagues and participating in teams ✅ ☐ ☐ 9 Professional conduct Respecting colleagues, partners, and the work environment ✅ ☐ ☐ 10 Problem-solving skills Identifying problems, proposing solutions, and showing creativity ☐ ✅ ☐ 11 Contribution to project/team Work effectiveness, innovative ideas, recognition from the team ✅ ☐ ☐ 12 Overall General evaluation of the entire internship period ✅ ☐ ☐ Areas for Improvement Need to build more confidence when communicating and presenting ideas in professional meetings. Continue to refine a more flexible problem-solving mindset, especially when dealing with complex issues in cloud systems. Optimize my ability to read and understand specialized English technical documentation to adopt new technologies even faster.

title: Week 6 Worklog
content: Week 6 Objectives: Master the 3-Tier application architecture (Web-App-DB) on AWS. Understand the data ingest, storage, and processing workflows using Kinesis and Glue. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 2 - Learn 3-Tier application architecture on AWS - VPC, Subnets, Security Groups, and Database Security Group 25/05/2026 25/05/2026 000005.awsstudygroup.com 3 - Create EC2 instance for Application Server - Create DB Subnet Group and deploy Amazon RDS 26/05/2026 26/05/2026 000005.awsstudygroup.com 4 - Deploy application to EC2 - Test connectivity between EC2 and RDS, perform data backup 27/05/2026 27/05/2026 000005.awsstudygroup.com 5 - Learn data ingest and store processes - Create S3 Bucket, IAM Role, and Kinesis Data Firehose 28/05/2026 28/05/2026 000035.awsstudygroup.com 6 - Create AWS Glue Crawler and Data Catalog - Test Data Lake, transform data, and visualize data 29/05/2026 29/05/2026 000040.awsstudygroup.com Week 6 Achievements: Deeply understood and hands-on deployed the classic highly secure 3-Tier web application architecture on AWS (VPC, Subnets, Security Groups). Successfully deployed a real-world application on EC2 as an Application Server and securely connected it to an Amazon RDS relational database. Grasped the big picture of Data Lakes and the high-speed data ingestion process using Kinesis Data Firehose targeting Amazon S3. Explored big data processing and visualization capabilities using AWS Glue Crawler and Data Catalog, preparing for in-depth analytics.

title: Sharing and feedback
content:
Overall Evaluation 1. Work Environment
During my time participating in the First Cloud Journey, I felt the learning and working environment was quite comfortable. The contents were organized week by week, making it easy for me to track my progress. Whenever I encountered something I did not understand, I could ask questions and continue practicing rather than being left behind. I also appreciated how the program encouraged learners to do labs independently, read documentation, and document results on their own, as this helped me retain knowledge for a longer time.
2. Support from Instructors/Group Administrators
The instructors and support team provided clear feedback whenever I encountered errors during labs or project deployment. I highly appreciated that people did not just give the answers outright, but often suggested directions for investigation so I could figure out the problem myself. This method of support helped me become more confident when working with the AWS Console, configuring services, and troubleshooting.
3. Relevance of the Work to My Major
The program’s content was highly relevant to my major, particularly in cloud computing, distributed systems, security, and data management. Through the Document Management System project, I had the opportunity to apply what I learned to a more realistic problem: user login, file uploads, metadata storage, access authorization, and audit logging.
4. Opportunities for Learning and Skill Development
I learned many additional AWS services such as S3, IAM, VPC, DynamoDB, Lambda, API Gateway, Cognito, and CloudFront. Beyond cloud knowledge, I also honed skills in reading documentation, writing worklogs, drafting reports, breaking down plans, and testing projects. These are skills I consider essential for future work in an enterprise environment.
5. Program Culture and Learning Spirit
I found that the program did a fairly good job of encouraging a self-learning spirit. Every week introduced new content, yet remained connected to previous weeks. When working on the final project, I gained a clearer understanding of why AWS services do not stand alone, but need to coordinate with each other to form a complete system.
6. Internship Policies and Benefits
The program provided clear documentation, labs, and orientation for each phase. Having AWS credits gave me the opportunity to practice directly on the cloud rather than just studying theory. I hope future iterations of the program will include a few sessions reviewing sample projects to help students better visualize the expected output standards.
Additional Questions What I Was Most Satisfied With During the Internship
What I was most satisfied with was getting hands-on experience building a project on AWS. While developing the DMS, I gained a clearer understanding of how the frontend, serverless backend, and cloud infrastructure connect with each other. Before this, I had only learned about individual services in isolation, but the project helped me see the big picture.
What the Company/Program Should Improve for Future Interns
I think the program could add an instructional session on how to present the final project, such as how to draw the architecture diagram, estimate costs, and demo the product. This would help interns better prepare for their final reports.
Would I Recommend This Program to Friends?
Yes. I will recommend this program to friends who want to learn about AWS and cloud computing. The program is suitable for beginners but still offers enough practical content for learners to progress week by week.
Suggestions and Expectations Suggestions for Improving the Internship Experience
I hope the program will provide a clear checklist for each week, including mandatory and optional extension parts. This way, learners can self-check their progress and know what additional tasks they can tackle if they have spare time.
Desire to Continue the Program in the Future
I want to continue participating in programs related to AWS, particularly in serverless, DevOps, and cloud security. After building the DMS, I realized I need to dive deeper into production deployment, system monitoring, and cost optimization.
Other Contributions/Feedback
First Cloud Journey provided me with a solid practical foundation in AWS. I did not just learn how to create services on the console; I also learned how to think in terms of system architecture: where data is stored, how users log in, how access rights are verified, and where costs might arise. This has been a very useful experience for my career orientation.

title: Week 7 Worklog
content: Week 7 Objectives: Get familiar with Amazon DynamoDB NoSQL database, practice data manipulation, and cost analysis. Explore DynamoDB Zero-ETL integrations. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 2 - Overview of Amazon DynamoDB - Tables, Items, Attributes, Primary Keys, and Secondary Indexes 01/06/2026 01/06/2026 000060.awsstudygroup.com 3 - Practice creating DynamoDB tables - Perform Create, Read, Update, Query operations and create a Global Secondary Index 02/06/2026 02/06/2026 000060.awsstudygroup.com 4 - Configure AWS CLI and AWS SDK for DynamoDB - Manipulate data using Python SDK (Boto3) 03/06/2026 03/06/2026 000011.awsstudygroup.com, 000039.awsstudygroup.com 5 - Build data for Cost & Usage Analysis - Analyze costs, usage, and tagging 04/06/2026 04/06/2026 000064.awsstudygroup.com 6 - Learn DynamoDB Zero-ETL - Connect OpenSearch Service and Amazon Bedrock for AI-powered search 05/06/2026 05/06/2026 000039.awsstudygroup.com Week 7 Achievements: Transitioned mindset from relational databases to NoSQL by familiarizing with the flexible architecture of Amazon DynamoDB. Proficiently practiced basic operations (Create, Read, Update, Query) and advanced indexing techniques (Global Secondary Index) on DynamoDB. Successfully integrated applications with the database using the AWS SDK (Boto3) in Python. Explored modern Zero-ETL technology, directly connecting data from DynamoDB to OpenSearch Service and Amazon Bedrock for AI-powered search applications.

title: Week 8 Worklog
content: Week 8 Objectives: Synthesize learned concepts to design a Serverless architecture for a Document Management System (DMS). Prepare the direction for the final project. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 2 - Synthesize DynamoDB and S3 knowledge - Determine applications for a document management system 08/06/2026 08/06/2026 3 - Learn CloudFront, API Gateway, and Lambda in a serverless architecture for an internal website 09/06/2026 09/06/2026 4 - Analyze Document Management System requirements - Login, upload, versioning, search, sharing, and audit logs 10/06/2026 10/06/2026 5 - Prepare Week 8 report documentation - Relate learned AWS concepts to the final DMS project 11/06/2026 11/06/2026 6 - Finalize Week 8 report - Review content, export PDF/DOCX, and prepare for the DMS project implementation 12/06/2026 12/06/2026 Week 8 Achievements: Gained the ability to synthesize core knowledge (S3, DynamoDB, Lambda, API Gateway) to conceptualize a complete Serverless system design. Deeply analyzed the business requirements of the final project (Document Management System - DMS), including authentication, authorization, and storage. Clearly understood the role of CloudFront in content delivery and API Gateway in securely routing API requests. Prepared ready-to-use profiles, design documents, and a clear roadmap to confidently begin implementing the final DMS project.

title: Week 9 Worklog
content: Week 9 Objectives: Analyze business requirements, design the AWS system architecture for the DMS project. Create a detailed deployment plan. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 2 - Analyze the DMS problem - Define users, departments, metadata, permissions, audit logs, and data risks 15/06/2026 15/06/2026 3 - Design AWS architecture for DMS - React, Cognito, API Gateway, Lambda, S3, DynamoDB, and CloudFront 16/06/2026 16/06/2026 4 - Establish file policies - Format allowlists, size limits, checksums, malware scanning, and quarantine buckets 17/06/2026 17/06/2026 5 - Create instruction.md, brainstorming.md, and analysis_plan.md to manage project scope and deployment plans 18/06/2026 18/06/2026 6 - Draw AWS/VPC architecture diagrams for DMS and prepare content for the Week 9 report 19/06/2026 19/06/2026 Week 9 Achievements: Completed the detailed analysis design (Analysis Plan) for the DMS system, clearly defining security risks and user roles. Finalized standard cloud architecture diagrams combining React, Cognito, API Gateway, Lambda, S3, and DynamoDB. Strictly established file security policies such as format allowlists, size limits, checksum verification, and Malware Scanning. Prepared a governance documentation system (instruction.md, brainstorming.md) as a guiding compass for the actual coding process.

title: Week 10 Worklog
content: Week 10 Objectives: Develop the DMS application (Frontend and Backend). Configure AWS Infrastructure as Code (IaC) using AWS CDK. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 2 - Initialize DMS monorepo - frontend React/Vite, aws/functions, aws/infrastructure, contracts, and docs 22/06/2026 22/06/2026 3 - Build React frontend - Document dashboard, filters, upload flows, protected routes, and document details 23/06/2026 23/06/2026 4 - Build AWS Lambda backend - /me, upload intents, documents, download intents, sharing, and audit events 24/06/2026 24/06/2026 5 - Build AWS CDK stack - Cognito, S3, DynamoDB, API Gateway, Lambda, CloudFront, SQS, SNS, and GuardDuty 25/06/2026 25/06/2026 6 - Run lint, typecheck, test, build, cdk synth, and complete the Week 10 report for the DMS project 26/06/2026 26/06/2026 Week 10 Achievements: Successfully initialized a Monorepo project structure, enabling unified and professional management of Frontend, Backend, and Infrastructure code. Developed a complete, intuitive React Frontend with secure authentication features and a user-friendly document management dashboard. Successfully developed all Serverless Backend APIs using AWS Lambda to handle upload, download, sharing logic, and audit logging. Deployed the entire infrastructure automatically as code (IaC) via AWS CDK, integrating SQS, SNS, and GuardDuty for high availability and security.

title: Week 11 Worklog
content: Week 11 Objectives: Finalize the DMS project, optimize security and costs. Prepare data for the system demonstration. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 2 - Finalize the DMS project - Review frontend, backend, OpenAPI contracts, and deployment documentation 29/06/2026 29/06/2026 3 - Security and cost checks - Budgets, IAM, private S3 buckets, CloudFront OAC, and CloudWatch/SNS alerts 30/06/2026 30/06/2026 4 - Prepare demo data - Sample Cognito users, HR/Tech/Sales documents, and upload/download/sharing scenarios 01/07/2026 01/07/2026 5 - Finalize reports, worklogs, and architectural presentation materials for the workshop 02/07/2026 02/07/2026 6 - Complete local end-to-end testing - Re-run lint, typecheck, test, build, cdk synth, and note key observations 03/07/2026 03/07/2026 Week 11 Achievements: Refined and comprehensively optimized the DMS system, ensuring smooth integration between Frontend, Backend, and OpenAPI contracts. Tightened system security by enforcing S3 Private Buckets, CloudFront OAC, and strict cost limits (AWS Budgets, CloudWatch). Created realistic demo data (HR/Tech/Sales scenarios) to vividly test and showcase document sharing workflows. Passed internal End-to-End tests, promptly fixing issues through rigorous linting, typechecking, and CDK synth processes.

title: Week 12 Worklog
content: Week 12 Objectives: Complete documentation, perform a final review of the entire DMS system. Submit the final project to AWS FCAJ. Tasks to be carried out this week: Day Task Start Date Completion Date Reference Material 2 - Prepare AWS FCAJ workshop submission package - Check repository, reports, worklogs, and instruction guides 06/07/2026 06/07/2026 3 - Final review of the DMS project - Test demo flows, architecture diagrams, policy files, and cost plans 07/07/2026 07/07/2026 4 - Finalize presentation content - Summarize the problem, AWS architecture, results, and remaining risks 08/07/2026 08/07/2026 5 - Package submission artifacts - Verify file names, formats, repository links, and proof documents 09/07/2026 09/07/2026 6 - Submit workshop to AWS FCAJ 10/07/2026 10/07/2026 Week 12 Achievements: Fully packaged the Document Management System, ready for operation in a real-world environment with high stability. Conducted a final comprehensive review of all business workflows (demo flows), design profiles, and detailed cost optimization plans. Built a professional presentation, concisely summarizing the problem, architectural solutions, and the project’s practical applicability. Successfully completed and submitted the final project report to the AWS FCAJ Workshop, marking a highly effective cloud learning journey.
